items.q: When a stress shifts an equilibrium, does the system fully undo the change?
items.correct: No — Le Chatelier's principle says the system PARTIALLY opposes the change; equilibrium shifts to a new position that minimises but doesn't eliminate the disturbance.
items.misconception: The system shifts to completely cancel the change and return concentrations to their original values.
items.random_wrong: The system shifts to amplify the change because reactions always proceed in the disturbed direction.
items.why: Students treat Le Chatelier as a perfect homeostat, missing that the new equilibrium reflects the new conditions and only partially counters the disturbance.

items.q: Does adding a catalyst change the value of Kc?
items.correct: No — a catalyst speeds up forward and reverse reactions equally, so Kc is unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst increases Kc by favouring product formation.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst decreases Kc by stabilising the reactants.
items.why: Students think catalysts 'help the reaction' so they must increase Kc; missing that Kc is determined by thermodynamics (ΔG), not by rate.

items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.

items.q: Increasing T on an exothermic reaction:
items.why: It's Decreases K. “Increases K” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: No, only faster equilibrium. “Yes, shifts right” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: For N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), if pressure is doubled, equilibrium shifts toward how many moles of gas?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
